1:
The witnesses are unanimous in reading the disciples' question — "Who is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?" — as the eruption of a carnal ambition that the whole chapter is framed to cure. They differ only in how they reconstruct its occasion and in what they draw from the child Jesus sets in their midst (v.2).

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Drawing on the parallels in Mark and Luke, they note the strife was old and recurrent, breaking out (as Lightfoot and Robertson observe) just after talk of Peter's prominence — the keys (16:19), the Transfiguration, the tax-coin paid for Peter — and again later (20:20–28; Luke 22:24). Robertson presses the comparative μείζων ("greater"), displacing the superlative, and Barnes, Ellicott and Clarke agree the apostles still dreamed of a temporal Messianic court with its chief offices; the primacy of Peter was plainly not yet conceded. Clarke even asks whether the question could have come from any but the nine who had not seen the Transfiguration, and finds in it proof that the disciples knew nothing of any Petrine supremacy.

The Reformed voices read it morally. Calvin, leaning on the other Evangelists, stresses that the disciples were dragged from their "lurking-places" — ambition concealed is ambition still — and marvels that the recent prediction of the cross was so quickly swallowed up, the flesh fastening on the one word of glory and forgetting the many of suffering; if the apostles so soon forgot, he warns, what shall become of us who dismiss the cross for idle speculation? Gill notes the eleven coming to the house after the coin was paid.

The Latin and Greek fathers, gathered in the Catena, treat the cure. Jerome, Chrysostom and the Gloss agree that Jesus, reading their thoughts, heals ambition by arousing "an emulation in lowliness," setting a child as the pattern of innocence — not of age but of freedom from malice, anger, and lust. Chrysostom tenderly notes that even the apostles' failings are above ours, for they asked who was greatest in heaven, not in the world, and that they soon laid this feeling aside. Henry, the Puritan, gathers the whole into his theme that Christ was the greatest both pattern and preacher of humility, and that Christ's answer "disallows and condemns the thing itself," leaving no room for a Petrine headship.

The notable outlier is Origen, who, beside the plain sense, ventures that the little one called and set in the midst may figure the Holy Spirit humbling himself in the disciples' hearts — an Alexandrian flight the others do not follow.
2:
On the simple act — Jesus calling a little child and setting him in the midst — the voices largely agree, dividing only over a late legend and over how strictly the child is to be imitated.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the scene. Robertson notes the child could walk, being called (προσκαλεσάμενος), and — since the talk was "in the house," probably Peter's (v.1, with Mark 9:33) — may even have been Peter's own child; he parses ἔστησεν as a transitive aorist ("set him") and adds from Mark and Luke that the child was placed "in the midst" and "by his side." Ellicott and he draw from Mark that Jesus first uttered the warning, "If any one wishes to be first, he shall be last of all and servant of all," and then folded the child in his arms. Ellicott reads the gesture as Christ's recognition of childhood's "gracious trust and freedom from rivalry," while "as yet the taint of egotism is undeveloped" — of a piece with 19:13 and 21:15–16.

The Reformed Calvin guards the image: the likeness is not at all points, for children have many native faults, and Paul bids us be children in malice but men in understanding (1 Corinthians 14:20), pressing on to the stature of perfect men (Ephesians 4:13); the one thing held up is that little children "know nothing about being preferred to each other, or about contending for the highest rank." He meets the objection that children too have a native pride by answering that comparisons must not be pressed at every point. Barnes, similarly, says Christ passes no verdict on children's native moral character but points only to their teachableness and lack of ambition, adding from Mark that the greatest must be "servant of all."

A small debate over a tradition divides the field: Clarke and Wesley repeat the story (from Nicephorus) that this child was Ignatius of Antioch, later cast to the beasts under Trajan; Ellicott reports the same Eastern legend but exposes its root in misreading Ignatius's self-title Θεοφόρος as passive ("God-borne") when Ignatius meant it actively ("God-bearing," one who carries God within); Robertson dismisses the ninth-century story as worthless and Gill calls it groundless, noting the chronology will not fit.
3:
"Except ye be converted, and become as little children" draws a settled agreement that the conversion meant is a turning from ambition, with a single debate over whether the saying reaches further to regeneration.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the verb. Ellicott insists the "conversion" is not yet the later half-technical sense but a plain turning from self-seeking to the relative blamelessness of children; the force of the words spoken to the Twelve "can hardly be exaggerated," for in the very dispute about precedence they showed they were "not truly in" the kingdom, whose first condition is the abnegation of self. Robertson parses στραφῆτε ("turn") as second-aorist passive and γένησθε as second-aorist middle, calls it a third-class condition, and notes the strong double negative οὐ μὴ — they were "headed in the wrong direction with their selfish ambition" and will otherwise "not get into the kingdom at all, let alone have big places in it." Both feel the markedly severe tone Bruce detected.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley press the moral turn from a temporal to a spiritual hope — lowly, helpless, knowing oneself ignorant and hanging wholly on the Father for every want; Clarke unfolds the heavenly kingdom whose King, subjects, country, and government are all spiritual. Here a real debate opens: Wesley adds, cautiously, that the text may also imply being "turned from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God," the inward renewal in God's image without which none enters the kingdom of glory — "thus must every man be converted in this life." The grammatical voices keep the saying to the narrower turn from ambition.

The Reformed Calvin links it to the example of v.2: the disciples were too habituated to the ordinary customs of men and must "wheel round" and get accustomed to a wholly different course, for Christ allots not even the lowest place to one who will not lose sight of distinctions. The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem reads the verse through its Johannine echo (John 3:5), making the new birth "of water and of the Spirit" the indispensable double cleansing — the water washing the body, the Spirit sealing the soul — so that neither without the other makes a man perfect; a baptismal turn the others do not develop here.
4:
"Whosoever shall humble himself as this little child, the same is greatest" — Christ's direct, surprising answer to the question of v.1 — unites the voices on a single paradox: greatness is reached only by self-abasement.

The grammatical-historical commentators guard the paradox against self-defeat. Ellicott warns that to "humble oneself" with the purpose of becoming great would frustrate itself and reduce humility to hypocrisy — the "pride that apes humility," the false lowliness of Colossians 2:18, being even more hateful than open self-assertion; the secret of true greatness lies in "that unconsciousness of being great" which takes the lowest place as its own by right. He notes the dramatic shift from plural to singular, picturing the child "shrinking timidly, with blushing face and downcast eyes." Robertson observes the comparative μείζων carried over from v.1, repeated by Christ elsewhere (Matthew 23:12), and that the child is humble "from the nature of the case" toward elders, however bumptious in himself; to humble oneself, says Bruce, is "the most difficult thing in the world for saint as for sinner." Barnes and Clarke agree there is no road to honour in this kingdom but humility and continual self-abasement, which saves its possessor from grasping at worldly honours or lordship over the brethren.

The Reformed Calvin supplies the definition: the truly humble man "neither claims any personal merit in the sight of God, nor proudly despises brethren," nor aims to be thought superior, but reckons it enough that he is one of the members of Christ and desires only that the Head be exalted — so that we never imagine we degrade ourselves by freely surrendering distinction. Gill, similarly, makes greatness belong to him who entertains mean thoughts of himself and condescends to those of the lowest state. The Alexandrian Origen offers his usual deeper register, measuring greatness by how far one has advanced toward the child's freedom from the affections and "sicknesses of the soul." Clarke adds that this disparity between Christ's kingdom and the kingdoms of this world means there is no way of rising to honour but by humility and continual self-abasement, which once held would keep a man from ever seeking to lord it over his brethren or to be "a lord in God's heritage."
5:
"Whoso shall receive one such little child in my name receiveth me" — the voices agree the "little child" is now read figuratively, of the humble believer, and that to receive such is to receive Christ himself.

The grammatical-historical commentators establish the sense. Robertson glosses ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματί μου as "on the ground of my name," "for my sake," the child standing as representative or symbol of the believer, and compares the kindred εἰς ὄνομα of Matthew 10:41. Barnes makes "receive" mean to approve, love, welcome, and aid in need one who has Christ's spirit, and links the saying forward to the law of judgment in Matthew 25:40; he and Clarke recall the episode Mark and Luke attach here (Mark 9:38), where John reports forbidding one who cast out devils in Christ's name and is corrected, since no one working a miracle in that name could lightly speak evil of him. Ellicott calls this saying the first utterance of that very law of final judgment, given "the widest possible range of universality" — no child of man excluded from those Christ calls his brethren.

The Reformed Calvin reads it as consolation: the term "little ones" is applied metaphorically to those who have laid aside lofty looks and conduct themselves with modesty, and Christ both takes them under his protection and commends them to the favour of men, teaching believers to esteem one another not as the children of the world maintain friendship — by flattering the proud — but by honouring those who willingly occupy the lowest place. Gill makes receiving them the embracing of their ministry and message with faith and love. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley makes it warmly personal — such little ones are "unspeakably dear to me; therefore help them all you can, as if it were myself in person," and see that you neither offend nor hinder them (Matthew 10:40; John 13:20). The Alexandrian Origen, characteristically, first allegorizes the manhood mortified — "bearing about in the body the putting to death of Jesus" — into a childlike freedom from sensual passion.
6:
The terrible saying — better a millstone and drowning than to offend one of these little ones — finds the voices agreed on its weight and its objects, their distinctive labours falling on the identity of the millstone and the geography of the punishment.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the philology. Robertson, Barnes, Ellicott and Clarke identify μύλος ὀνικός as the large "ass-millstone," the upper stone turned by a donkey, not the small handmill of Luke 17:35; Robertson adds that the adjective ὀνικός, unattested in classical Greek, now turns up in the papyri for loads needing an ass to carry or move them, and that the depth "of the sea" (τῷ πελάγει) names the open, beating waves — a vivid "far out" beyond mere drowning. Lightfoot, Clarke and Ellicott note the punishment, though not a Jewish penalty, was used by Greeks and Romans (Ellicott citing its infliction by Augustus and Jerome's claim that it was practised in Galilee, perhaps on the followers of Judas of Galilee), so the words would come home with special vividness. Ellicott and Barnes mark how Mark and Luke insert here John's complaint about the exorcist (Mark 9:38), so that John was unwittingly "offending" one who believed.

The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as consolation and warning together: it comforts the godly who, choosing modesty, fear they expose themselves to contempt — God does not despise them — and it threatens the proud, who, thinking too highly of themselves, trample the weak; to "offend" is to cause a brother to stumble, to draw him aside from the right course, or to retard him in it, recalling Paul's charge to condescend to men of low estate (Romans 12:16). Gill takes the little ones as those great in grace, gifts, and usefulness, yet little in their own eyes and despicable to the world. The Latin fathers add the millstone's allegory — Hilary making the mill the blind, circular toil of Gentile ignorance, Gregory the dull round of earthly action that binds the neck to vain desires. The Alexandrian Origen reads the "little one" as the newly born in Christ, still capable of being made to stumble before he is fully made like a child.
7:
"Woe unto the world because of offences! for it must needs be that offences come; but woe to that man by whom the offence cometh" — the voices wrestle the great paradox: stumbling-blocks are inevitable, yet the one through whom they come is guilty.

The grammatical-historical commentators hold the two truths in tension. Ellicott states it sharply: crimes seem to recur "with something like the inevitable regularity of a law," and yet in each single instance the offender's will is free to choose, and he is rightly held responsible by divine and human laws alike. Robertson: Jesus recognizes the inevitableness of the traps, hindrances, and snares "the world being as it is," but does not absolve the man who sets them (Luke 17:1). Barnes and Clarke ground the necessity in human depravity — ἀνάγκη γάρ ἐστιν, "the coming of offences is unavoidable" — Clarke adding that "woe" (οὐαί) here carries our Lord's sympathy and concern as much as denunciation, and warns that he who gives the offence and he who takes it are both exposed to ruin.

The fathers, with one voice, defend providence against fatalism. Chrysostom insists the prophecy does not cause the offences: "it is not done because He foretold it, but He foretold it because it was certainly to come to pass"; the necessity is moral, not absolute, for the saying "If thy hand offend thee" shows there is no compulsion. The Gloss reads the "need" through 1 Corinthians 11:19 — they come that the approved may be made manifest. Origen casts the offences as the army of Satan, his angels seeking out instruments, on whom falls the heavier woe, worse than that of Tyre and Sidon. Augustine's pastoral John Chrysostom answers the objection that Christ ought rather to heal than lament: to foretell and to bewail is itself a kind of healing, for "many oftentimes… when mourned for, amended."

The Reformed Calvin gathers it: there is nothing we should dread more than offences, since Satan, holding innumerable kinds of them, throws new difficulties in our way at almost every step, and few persevere to the end; God wills to leave his people exposed to them to exercise their faith and to sift the chaff from the wheat (again 1 Corinthians 11:19) — yet the guilt rests wholly on the offender. The Latin Hilary and Jerome narrow the "man" to Judas, whose soul was made ready for betrayal, and to the Jewish people who took offence at the lowliness of the cross.
8:
"If thy hand or thy foot offend thee, cut it off, and cast it from thee" — the voices recognize this as a saying Christ repeats from the Sermon (Matthew 5:29–30), and read it of whatever, however dear, occasions sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the repetition and weigh the words. Robertson catalogues this as one of Matthew's "doublets" of Matthew 5:29–30, observes that for εἰς γέενναν the verse now reads εἰς τὸ πῦρ τὸ αἰώνιον with τοῦ πυρός added at the end of v.9, marks the first Matthean use of αἰώνιος ("ageless, without beginning or end, as of God," Romans 16:26), and argues pointedly that the effort to make it mean a temporary fire would equally shorten the life: "if the punishment is limited, the life is shortened." He also notes the rare compound μονόφθαλμον ("one-eyed"). Barnes draws the doctrinal weight: the maimed-but-saved figure is not about the resurrection body — which will be perfect (1 Corinthians 15:42–44) — but is conclusive proof that the alternative is everlasting fire, so that "the sufferings of the wicked will be eternal." Clarke simply refers the reader back to the Sermon.

Chrysostom, cited in the Catena on v.7, had already turned the limbs into friends dear to us as our own members yet harmful — "nothing is so hurtful as evil communications" — making the saying a warning against ruinous companionship rather than the mutilation of the body. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley follows exactly: "the most beloved and useful person" who turns us out of, or hinders us in, the way must be parted with; "Is not this a hard saying? Yes; if thou take counsel with flesh and blood." The Alexandrian Origen presses the body-of-the-church image: as the eye cannot rightly say to a sound hand "I have no need of thee," so when a hand or foot — a gifted minister or member — becomes a stumbling-block to the whole body, the head may say it, cut it off, and cast it away, that the rest of the body enter into life maimed rather than perish whole.
9:
The witnesses on this verse are few and brief, treating "if thine eye offend thee, pluck it out and cast it from thee" as the simple completion of the preceding saying rather than a fresh theme — a clear instance where the cloud of witnesses falls almost silent because all has been said on v.8.

The grammatical-historical Barnes merely cross-refers to v.8, where the meaning is given in full: it is better to enter into life one-eyed than, having two eyes, to be cast into hell-fire; the figure of the maimed body is not literal of the resurrection state but a vivid way of teaching that the dearest occasion of sin must be surrendered rather than the soul be lost. Robertson's notes on the αἰώνιον fire and on the rare word μονόφθαλμον ("one-eyed"), gathered under v.8, bear directly here, since this is the verse in which that compound stands. The Reformed-and-grammatical Jamieson-Fausset-Brown add only the briefest pointer to the law standing behind the wider context (Deuteronomy 5:16; Exodus 21:17), honouring father and mother and the penalty on those who curse them.

The thin comment is itself instructive: where the voices repeat without expansion, the synthesis honestly reports a doublet, not a new doctrine. Christ's repeated demand — for radical, costly mortification of whatever leads to sin, weighed always against the eternal fire — is the single, shared note, and the witnesses are content to let the fuller treatment of v.8 carry the verse. Calvin's and Henry's fuller handling of this radical surgery is gathered at v.8, where the same demand was first weighed; here the witnesses simply let the warning stand. The single, shared, grounded note is that no occasion of sin, however precious, is worth the loss of the soul — "it is better for thee to enter into life with one eye, than having two eyes to be cast into hell-fire" (Mark 9:47) — and that the figure of the maimed body, repeated from the Sermon on the Mount, presses upon disciple and sinner alike the costliness of holiness and the dreadful reality of the αἰώνιον fire of which the next clause speaks.
10:
"Take heed that ye despise not one of these little ones; for… their angels do always behold the face of my Father" — the voices agree the verse forbids contempt and grounds the prohibition in heaven's care, but they divide over guardian angels.

The chief debate concerns whether each believer has a single guardian angel. Clarke holds Christ here "establishes the notion received by almost all nations" of a personal guardian with constant access to God, citing the rabbinic "angels of the face" (malakey panim) and the eastern privilege of seeing the king's face (Esther 1:14). Ellicott agrees the words "distinctly recognise the belief in guardian angels," entrusted with a definite work and ranked among the highest of the host, the "angels of the Presence" like Gabriel (Luke 1:19), at a time when the Sadducees were denying the ministry of angels (Acts 23:8). The Reformed Calvin sharply denies the inference: the words do not bear that one angel watches one man continually; Scripture rather assigns many angels to guard the godly (Psalm 34:7), so "away with the fanciful notion of a good and evil angel," the care of the whole Church being committed to the angelic host. Robertson stands between, noting the Jewish belief in national angels (Daniel 10:13) and leaving the personal question open, while affirming the comfort that the Father takes special care of his little ones (Hebrews 1:14).

The fathers add their own riches. Origen canvasses three views — the angel given at regeneration, or from birth to the foreknown, or one who himself advances as the believer advances — while warning that the perfect are not easily scandalized. Gregory, citing Dionysius, says these come from the lesser ranks of angels, yet even when sent they "always behold the face of God," coming forth without leaving the inward contemplation. Augustine clarifies that they are "our" angels because they have begun to have us for fellow-citizens, the "face of God" meaning his self-manifestation, not a bodily feature, the vision John promises (1 John 3:2). The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem qualifies the sight: even angels behold God only as far as they are able, not as he is, for none has seen the Father save the Son (John 6:46). The chapter's mounting argument — angels, then the Son (v.11), then the Father (v.14) — begins here.
11:
"For the Son of man is come to save that which was lost" — the voices treat this as the second and stronger reason not to despise the little ones, while several flag a textual question.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the criticism. Ellicott reports the verse "wanting in many of the best MSS." but, assuming its genuineness, draws two points: the angels' ministry shares in the Son's saving work, the highest expression of the Eternal Father's will, recalling the promise that Nathanael should see the angels ascending and descending upon the Son of Man (John 1:51) — "not only doing homage to His glory, but helping Him in His work"; and the words exclude any thought that the child had special saintliness — his claim was simply "his need and his capacity for all that is implied in salvation," as true of any "wastrel child of the streets" as of the holiest. Gill and Clarke both note the bracketing of the verse in some copies but defend it from the undisputed parallel (Luke 19:10), which has "not a single various reading"; Clarke draws out the wordplay between ἀπολωλός, the lost, and Ἀπολλύων, the Destroyer (Revelation 9:11) — Satan the loser and the lost, but the Son come to save the lost.

The Reformed Calvin makes the argument personal to Christ: he came down from heaven to save not the living only but "even the dead"; it is wholly unreasonable to disdain those whom the Son of God so highly esteemed, whom we are to value "not for the value of their virtues, but for the sake of Christ," and he who will not conform to Christ's example is "too saucy and proud." Gill expands the chain: if God so regarded these little ones as to send his Son to obtain eternal salvation for them when they were not only little but lost, they are far above any mortal's contempt. Barnes and the wesleyan Wesley sound the same note — the strongest reason of all is that Christ himself came to seek and save them. The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem turns to the cross willingly embraced (Matthew 20:18), the Friend of man shunning not death "lest the whole world should perish in its sins."
12:
The parable of the hundred sheep — "doth he not leave the ninety and nine… and seeketh that which is gone astray?" — draws agreement that it grounds the care owed to the straying little one, with the voices pointing to its fuller form in Luke.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the text and the parallel. Robertson prefers the reading "leave the ninety and nine upon the mountains" (the high pastures where sheep graze at will) and notes the present tense of "seek" for the wandering one; he, Barnes and Ellicott all refer to the richer telling in Luke 15:4–6. Ellicott reads the "ninety and nine" as strictly the unfallen creatures of God's spiritual universe, and relatively those comparatively free from gross offence; the opening "How think ye?" sharpens the appeal to the disciples' own experience and asserts Christ's own claim to be the true Shepherd. Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic idiom of "ninety-nine… and one," common in the Talmud. Clarke, comparing Luke 15:4 ("in the desert"), takes the scene as Christ's constant solicitude for the scattered country folk, "sheep without a shepherd" (Matthew 9:36), neglected by the scribes — and reads it also as comfort to backsliders: "How hardly does Christ give them up!"

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes the two Evangelists' aims: in Matthew the lesson is that a good teacher must labour to recover the wandering disciple, bearing with the imperfections of those over whom God has set his Son as shepherd, gathering rather than driving them off; in Luke (set against the Pharisees' murmuring at Christ's eating with sinners) the point is that the whole human race belongs to God, so that we ought to gather the strayed and rejoice over their return as a man recovering a lost treasure. Gill notes the one difference between shepherd and Christ — finding is uncertain for the man, certain for Christ. The Alexandrian Origen, unusually, passes over this verse here, candidly referring his readers to his Homilies on Luke — an editorial note rather than an interpretation. Lightfoot multiplies the rabbinic parallels — "if ninety-nine say, scatter them, and only one, divide them, they hearken to him" — to show how natural the idiom was to Christ's hearers, and Wesley, characteristically brief, simply refers the reader to the fuller form in Luke.
13:
"He rejoiceth more of that sheep, than of the ninety and nine which went not astray" — the comment is brief, the voices content to register the joy of recovery, with the fuller weight reserved for the Lukan parallel (Luke 15:7).

The grammatical-historical commentators stay close to the words. Clarke notes it is natural for a person "to express unusual joy at the fortunate accomplishment of an unexpected event" — the surprise and the fear of loss heighten the gladness. Ellicott refines only the grammar, reading "rejoiceth over it," and points, as on the previous verse, to the parallel in Luke for the full exposition, where the joy in heaven over one repenting sinner is set above that over ninety-nine just persons who need no repentance.

The Reformed Gill marks the one point at which the comparison breaks down between the shepherd and Christ: for the man, the finding is "casual and uncertain," but not so with Christ, "who certainly finds all those he goes after, and seeks" — grounding the joy not in chance but in the sureness of the Good Shepherd's pursuit. The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem, on the theme of eternal life rather than the verse itself, gathers proof-texts on how that life is gained — by faith (John 3:36) and by the preaching of the gospel — a tangential note characteristic of how the Catena and catechetical excerpts sometimes attach to a verse by association rather than direct exposition. The shared and grounded note among all the voices is simply this: heaven's gladness is greatest over the one recovered. Barnes, like the others, sends the reader to the parable's fuller exposition in Luke (Luke 15:4–7), where its meaning is unfolded at length; the witnesses are content here to let the single image of the recovered sheep, and the joy that crowns its finding, do its quiet work, the heaped-up tenderness of God toward the one strayed believer being the chapter's running theme since v.10. The brevity of the comment is itself faithful to the text, which adds little the parable of the strayed sheep has not already taught, and the witnesses do not pad it.
14:
"Even so it is not the will of your Father which is in heaven, that one of these little ones should perish" — the application of the parable and, as Wesley notes, the top of the chapter's gradation: the angels, the Son, the Father.

The grammatical-historical commentators feel the force of the negative. Ellicott says the form "it is not the will" has all the force of the rhetorical use of the negative — it suggests "the will of the Father is the very opposite of that," so the words are identical in teaching with Paul's "He will have all men to be saved" (1 Timothy 2:4); he notes too the continued presence of the child in "one of these little ones." Robertson flags the textual variant between "your" and "my" Father — reading μου with B but observing that "your" carries on the picture of God's care for "each one of these little ones" — and notes the Hebraism ἔμπροσθεν, "before the face," with θέλημα, as in Matthew 11:25. Barnes links the verse forward to 2 Peter 3:9.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws the doctrine plainly: if any soul is finally lost, "it is not because God's will or counsel was against its salvation," nor that a proper provision had not been made, but because, though light came into the world, it "preferred darkness to light, because of its attachment to its evil deeds." Gill, the Reformed voice, climbs the same gradation — angels, then the Son, then the sovereign will of the Father — but locates the security of the little ones precisely in "the sovereign will" of the Father as "the source and security of their everlasting happiness." The two stances meet in the text yet lean differently: Clarke toward a universal saving will resistible by man, Gill toward the effectual will securing the elect little ones — without either denying that the Father wills none of these to perish (2 Peter 3:9). Henry binds the whole gradation together — "the angels, the Son, the Father" — observing that Christ calls God "my Father" in v.19 but "your Father" here, since he is not ashamed to call his poor disciples brethren; their safety is that God is their Father, a place of prospect, who sees every indignity offered them, and a place of power, who is able to avenge them.
15:
"If thy brother shall trespass against thee, go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone" — the voices agree this opens the law of brotherly correction, and read it as rooted in Leviticus 19:17, "thou shalt surely rebuke thy neighbour, and not suffer sin upon him."

The fathers dwell on the spirit of the reproof. Chrysostom marks how Christ devises a new method — bringing the wronged man to the wrongdoer, since the offender, held by shame, would not readily come — and that he says not "accuse" or "chide" or "demand redress" but "tell him his fault," as a physician visiting a man in a deep slumber; the aim is correction, not revenge, the private setting chosen "that you may have witnesses that you have done all your part," and the gain is mutual, for "both of you had suffered loss." Augustine insists the rebuke must be "in love, not eager to wound, but anxious to amend": forget your own wrong but not your brother's wound, and "if you alone know of his trespass, and proceed to rebuke him before all, you do not correct but betray him" — private sins privately, public sins before all (with 1 Timothy 5:20). Jerome and the Gloss trace the escalating steps, and Raban warns we forgive only the one who will hear and repent, lest sufferance be "too far relaxed."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices supply the legal and verbal frame. Lightfoot grounds the duty in the Talmud's reading of Leviticus 19:17 — reprove "even to blows" — but says Christ requires "a higher charity," from the offended party. Gill restricts the rule to brethren and church members, not those "without." Henry expounds it at length as the rule for preserving both the peace and the purity of the church, taking ἔλεγξον as "argue the case with him… with reason and argument, not with passion," and reading "thou hast gained thy brother" as "the winning of that soul" (Proverbs 11:30; James 5:19–20).

A real debate surfaces over scope: Origen asks whether the precept covers all sins, or only lesser ones — since grave sinners (the fornicator, idolater, murderer) are, with Paul (1 Corinthians 5:11), scarcely "brethren" at all, and ought rather to be put out at once — and leaves the tension candidly open, warning against both a laxity that emboldens sin and a severity that despairs of the offender, since judgment whether he finally perishes belongs to God.
16:
"But if he will not hear thee, then take with thee one or two more, that in the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established" — the second step, which the voices unanimously root in the law of witnesses, Deuteronomy 19:15.

The grammatical-historical commentators set out its purpose. Barnes lists two ends: the added persons may induce the offender to listen where one could not, and they serve as witnesses of his conduct before the church should the matter go further; they should be "persons of influence" or trusted friends. Ellicott and Clarke stress they must not be partisans but "disinterested representatives" of what is likely to be the common moral estimate of the fault, held in reserve as witnesses "that every effort has been made in the spirit of a righteous friendship" — Ellicott noting that this drawing of the Law's highest ethical teaching into the gospel is a feature of our Lord's method too little recognised (John 8:17). Robertson glosses παράλαβε μετὰ σοῦ simply, "take along with thee." Lightfoot illustrates from rabbinic and Talmudic practice, where the offender brought others to appease the wronged party at the sepulchre if he had died, but observes that Christ "requires a higher charity" — laid upon the offended, not the offender.

The Reformed Calvin adds a discriminating caution that keeps the step from misfiring: it assumes obstinacy, not flat denial. One who explicitly denies the fact and claims he is falsely accused "must be left alone," for the presence of witnesses will only provoke a more wicked denial; but those who shamelessly evade or impudently excuse a known wrong are rightly pressed by the greater authority of two or three. He recalls that the verb ἔλεγξον means "to convince by demonstration… to show by good reasons," and that Christ, alluding to Moses, turns the law of establishing a matter by witnesses to the further purpose of giving the second admonition "greater weight and impressiveness," till the church itself may take cognizance. Henry adds three reasons for the second step: to assist the reprover with a wiser word, to affect the offender who will be more humbled when he sees it witnessed against by two or three, and to be witnesses of his conduct should the matter come before the church — for none should be censured as obstinate "till it be very well proved that they are so."
17:
"And if he shall neglect to hear them, tell it unto the church; but if he neglect to hear the church, let him be unto thee as a heathen man and a publican" — the voices converge on the final step of discipline, then divide sharply over the meaning of "the church" and the treatment of the excluded.

The great debate is over ἐκκλησία. Ellicott mounts a famous protest: the word truly means "congregation" or "assembly," as every English version from Tyndale rendered it until the Rhemish "church," and the appeal is to the society at large — "the public opinion of the Ecclesia is to be brought to bear upon the offender" — not to its rulers; whole "theories of church authority… have been built upon" the later mistranslation, and he traces how the bishops in time took the place of the congregation. Robertson takes it as the local body (distinct from the universal sense of Matthew 16:18) and weighs whether an actual congregation or one prophetically foreseen is meant, noting some think the Twelve were a "moving church" of preachers. Gill ventures the apostles themselves were the "great congregation" then in being. Barnes allows either the whole assembly or its appointed representatives, as in the synagogue's bench of elders.

The Reformed Calvin argues Christ borrowed the existing Jewish form — the Sanhedrin's power of excommunication, lawful and approved by God — and "conveyed to us" what had been preserved under the Law, so that gospel discipline succeeds the ancient, lodged not in one man but in elders. On the second clause the voices soften the severity: Barnes, Ellicott and Henry agree "as a heathen and a publican" means to cease religious communion, not kindness; he is to be regarded "as one in a capacity of being restored and received in again," not as an enemy but admonished as a brother, Paul's handling of the Corinthian offender (1 Corinthians 5:1–5; 2 Corinthians 2:6–7) being the pattern. Jerome alone sharpens it — the believer who lives as an unbeliever is more to be abhorred than open Gentiles, and "publicans" are those who pursue worldly gain by fraud.
18:
"Verily I say unto you, Whatsoever ye shall bind on earth shall be bound in heaven" — the same words once given to Peter (Matthew 16:19) are now extended to all the disciples, and the voices divide over what "binding and loosing" means.

A clear debate runs through the comments. Most Christian interpreters, says Clarke, restrain it to matters of discipline and authority — and so Jerome, Hilary, Chrysostom and the Gloss read it: the apostolic sentence of excommunication and absolution is ratified by the sentence of God, so that the condemned man cannot retort "I will despise you in return." Yet Clarke himself, following Lightfoot, urges "as plain as the sun" the rabbinic usage in which to bind is to declare a thing unlawful and to loose to declare it lawful — a declaratory power over things (the neuter ὅσα, "whatsoever"), not persons — citing the formula by which a Jewish teacher took "liberty to teach what is bound and what is loose." Ellicott blends the two: here the words "approximate more closely" to condemning and absolving, since the Christian had to apply general laws to particular cases, binding or loosing "directly as interpreting the Law, only secondarily and indirectly as punishing or pardoning."

The fathers press its pastoral edge. Chrysostom notes Christ says not "bind such a man" but "if ye shall bind," leaving it to discretion, and sets the incorrigible under a twofold necessity, the present casting-out and the future judgment, thus lessening the brother's wrath. Augustine warns the binding must be "with just cause; for an unjust cause breaks rightful bonds," and that to loose on earth (by correction and agreement) is to confer a boon on the offender, who harmed himself, not us. Origen observes the wording differs from Peter's — "in heaven," not "in the heavens" — "for they are not yet attained to the like perfection with Peter." The Reformed Calvin and the grammatical Barnes both restrict the promise: it confirms not every decision but only that which proceeds from Christ's word and Spirit — Barnes denying it extends to any but the apostles, since all others may err — and Calvin turning it against "the Papists" who "torture this passage to cloak every species of tyranny," insisting the lawful government of the Church belongs to elders, not to a "mitered and disguised beast."
19:
"Again I say unto you, That if two of you shall agree on earth as touching any thing that they shall ask, it shall be done for them of my Father which is in heaven" — the voices read the promise in close connection with binding and loosing, and disagree mainly over its breadth.

The grammatical-historical commentators tie it tightly to its context. Barnes limits the promise to the apostles organizing and establishing the church (citing Acts 1 and Acts 15), denying it "can, with any propriety, be applied to the ordinary prayers of believers" — other promises cover those (Mark 11:24; 1 John 5:14). Robertson and Clarke savour the verb συμφωνήσωσιν, our "symphony": Clarke hears "a number of musical instruments set to the same key, and playing the same tune," a concord of hearts as pleasing to God's ear as a good concert to ours, and links the joint prayer directly to the binding and loosing just commanded — "what power faithful prayer has with God!" Ellicott guards it with the implied conditions of v.20 — prayer in Christ's name, in entire submission to the Father's will — lest men ask amiss, as the sons of Zebedee did (Matthew 20:20), and have the request withheld or granted otherwise than they wished.

The Reformed Calvin and the Puritan Henry read it more broadly while keeping its discipline-context. Calvin makes it a remarkable promise to public prayer, joining counsel sought at God's mouth with united supplication, so that God both ratifies decisions of which he is himself the Author and graciously listens to the common requests of his people, teaching moderation and humility in all religious acts. Henry applies it both "in general, to all the requests of the faithful praying seed of Jacob" — no law of heaven limiting the number of petitioners, God being especially pleased with their union and communion (Acts 4:31) — and "in particular" to prayers for binding and loosing, insisting prayer must always accompany discipline: "pass no sentence which you cannot in faith ask God to confirm." The Alexandrian Origen, characteristically, hears in the "agreement" (συμφωνία) the harmony of the two covenants, joined by the Spirit as a third.
20:
"For where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them" — the voices unite in hearing here a claim to Christ's divine, special presence with even the smallest assembly.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the divinity. Barnes and Clarke argue the saying is impossible for any but God: at the same moment thousands of assemblies may be gathered in his name across the globe — "in Greenland, in Africa, in Ceylon… in almost every part of the world" — and Christ is in the midst of each; "can any… say these words, except that God who fills both heaven and earth?" Clarke gathers Jewish sayings (the Shechinah among those who study the Law) and even a Hindu parallel about the divine presence in worship, only to set Christ's word above them, adding that he is present "not to spy out their sins… but to enlighten, strengthen, comfort, and save." Robertson and Ellicott note an apocryphal Oxyrhynchus saying ("raise the stone and there thou shalt find me") and the patristic axiom Ubi tres, ibi Ecclesia — "where three are, there is a church" — the strength of the society measured not by number but by fulfilling "the true conditions of its life." Both stress the qualifier "in my name": gathered as disciples, by his authority and appointment, for his worship (1 Corinthians 5:4).

The Reformed Calvin makes this promise wider than the last: Christ will be present to guide his people by his counsel (Psalm 73:24) and to bring their undertakings to a prosperous result — a spur to unite in piety and holiness, since whoever despises the holy assemblies or separates from the brethren shows he sets no value on Christ's presence; yet Calvin warns the name is wickedly claimed by ungodly councils, against whose "bastard and abortive" decisions only the sincere worshippers of God may claim the promise. The Antiochene Chrysostom adds that Christ, the chief motive of love to neighbour, will be present even where virtue is shown toward others, and answers why some who "agree" yet go unheard — they ask things inexpedient, or fail to bring their own part, or seek vengeance, or beg mercy for the unrepentant. Origen and Wesley locate the presence spiritually: "not going to be… but at the very moment of the concord being Himself found," by his Spirit quickening their prayers and guiding their counsels. Henry calls it the equivalent of the Shechinah of tabernacle and temple (Exodus 40:34).
21:
Peter's question — "Lord, how oft shall my brother sin against me, and I forgive him? till seven times?" — arises, the voices agree, straight out of the foregoing teaching on the offending brother, and shows how little even Peter had grasped the boundlessness of mercy.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the Jewish background. Barnes, Clarke, Lightfoot and Robertson agree the rabbis taught forgiveness three times but not the fourth; Peter "more than doubled this" and thought himself generous — Ellicott hearing a half-conscious echo of "for three transgressions and for four" (Amos 1:3) as the natural limit. Robertson, with Plummer, lands the verdict: "the man who asks such a question does not really know what forgiveness means." Lightfoot exposes the "snake" beneath the seemingly large rabbinic charity — they pardon once, twice, thrice, "fourthly, they do not pardon him." Jamieson-Fausset-Brown only mark the "ruinous effects of erroneous teaching" behind such reckoning.

The fathers turn at once to number-symbolism (developed under v.22): Jerome reads "seventy times seven" as four hundred and ninety, Augustine boldly extends it — "if he shall sin a hundred times… forgive him," for "if Christ found a thousand sins, yet forgave them all" — appealing to Colossians 3:13, "forgive… even as God in Christ forgave you," and unfolding the symbolism of ten (the Law), eleven (transgression), and seven (completeness). Origen warns against a wooden arithmetic that would forgive to the seventy-seventh sin but not the seventy-eighth — "altogether silly and unworthy alike" of Peter's progress and of "the divine magnanimity of Jesus." Raban distinguishes pardon granted a penitent brother (as Joseph to his brethren) from the goodwill owed even a hostile foe (as David mourning Saul).

The Reformed Calvin reads Peter "according to the natural feelings… of the flesh," fearing that easy pardon will only "induce others to do ill"; but Christ, so far from yielding, sets no limit at all. Calvin then distinguishes two kinds of forgiveness — the love that lays aside revenge even toward the impenitent, repaying kindness for injury, and the reconciliation that receives the penitent into favour — and, weighing Luke's added "if he repent" (Luke 17:4), holds that we owe goodwill always but admit to full reconciliation on "probable evidence" of repentance, neither with a foolish credulity nor with a cruel rigour that drives the wretched to despair.
22:
"Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times: but, Until seventy times seven" — the voices agree Christ names not a ceiling but boundlessness, and note the deliberate counter-echo of Lamech.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the arithmetic and the allusion. Robertson observes the idiom ἕως ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά is ambiguous — seventy-seven, or, as the Revisers take it, four hundred and ninety — and that the same ambiguity attends the Septuagint of Genesis 4:24; but "it really makes little difference because Jesus clearly means unlimited forgiveness in either case," the "unlimited revenge of primitive man" giving place to "the unlimited forgiveness of Christians." Clarke and Ellicott both hear the pointed reversal of that very text, Lamech's boast that he should be "avenged seventy and sevenfold"; Ellicott adds that the use of the symbolic numbers of completeness was "obviously designed to lead the mind of the questioner altogether away from any specially numerical standard," for as there is no limit to God's forgiveness there should be none to man's. Clarke, more literally, reckons that four hundred and ninety genuine, unprovoked offences could scarcely be received by anyone in a whole lifetime, and that where the offended has given cause, half the offence at least ceases.

The Antiochene Chrysostom agrees Christ "does not limit a definite number within which forgiveness must be kept," but "signifies thereby something endless and ever enduring," just as "the barren hath borne seven" means many; the number stands for the infinite and perpetual. The wesleyan Wesley sums the whole: "that is, as often as there is occasion. A certain number is put for an uncertain." The cloud is here entirely of one mind — the disciple's forgiveness, like God's, knows no tally. Gill notes the same insistence on the unlimited from the Jewish side, that the offended is "forbidden to be difficult to pardon, for that is not the manner of the seed of Israel" — a maxim the rabbis nonetheless capped at three, which Christ here bursts open; and the cloud is wholly agreed that Christ shifts the question off any tally altogether, making forgiveness as boundless toward the brother as the Father's is toward us.
23:
The parable of the unmerciful servant opens — "Therefore is the kingdom of heaven likened unto a certain king, which would take account of his servants" — and the voices read it as Christ's confirmation that we must forgive without limit.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the realia. Barnes and Clarke take the "servants" not as domestics but as petty kings or revenue-farmers — only such could owe so vast a sum, for kings often farmed out the taxes of a province — and read the "reckoning" as the day of account when every man gives answer to God (Romans 14:12), every sin being a debt contracted with divine justice. Robertson notes συναῖραι λόγον, "to cast up accounts, to settle," found in second-century papyri and an ostracon. Ellicott marks the parable as in some sense an advance on those of Matthew 13:3, more fully bringing out human interests after the manner of Luke.

The Reformed Calvin locates the whole force in three contrasts — the king against the servant, the immense sum against the small or ordinary sums, extreme kindness against extreme cruelty: "what are we, if we are compared with God? And how large is the sum which every one of us owes to God?" He who, oppressed with an immense load himself, "implacably refuses to forgive even the smallest offenses," judges very ill for himself, for God will be "equally stern and inexorable" toward him. The Latin fathers moralize the figures: Augustine and Raban read the ten thousand talents as all sins committed under the Ten Commandments; Remigius makes the king both Father and Son, the servant all mankind created for God's praise and given the law of nature, the "wife" his folly; Chrysostom dwells on the king's "unspeakable tenderness," giving more than was asked — a full cancelling — yet taking account first "that he should at the least be made more merciful to his fellow servants." The chief outlier is Origen, who first refers the reckoning to the consummation when the King returns with his kingdom (Matthew 25:19), then mounts a Christological flight — the King is the Son, who is himself absolute Wisdom, Righteousness, Truth, and Kingdom — a register the plainer voices do not share, even as Jerome explicitly rejects reading the debtor as the devil.
24:
"And when he had begun to reckon, one was brought unto him, which owed him ten thousand talents" — the voices spend themselves chiefly on the staggering size of the debt, agreeing it figures the immensity of what we owe God.

The grammatical-historical commentators reckon the sum, and their very disagreements underscore its vastness. Barnes computes it, if silver, at over sixteen million dollars, which "proves that he was not a domestic, but some tributary prince," the figure showing that "our sins are so great that they cannot be estimated or numbered." Clarke makes μυρίων ταλάντων "the highest number known in Greek arithmetical notation," an immense sum — if gold, sixty-seven million pounds, "equal to the annual revenue of the British empire." Ellicott, working from the Septuagint's use of "talent" for the Hebrew kikar, sets it at about two and a half million sterling, "named in its vague vastness to indicate the immensity of the debt which man owes to God," the ever-accumulating aggregate of sins of omission and commission brought home when God "takes account." Robertson compares the whole imperial taxes of Judea, Idumea and Samaria — only six hundred talents a year, Galilee and Perea two hundred — marvelling at "an enormous sum for that period," while noting oriental kings ran up such debts freely. Wesley likewise intimates "the vast number and weight of our offences against God, and our utter incapacity of making him any satisfaction."

The Alexandrian Origen, true to form, reads the moment morally: the reckoning "begins" at the house of God (1 Peter 4:17), "like the twinkling of an eye," and the man owed many talents because, "entrusted with great things" and "having had many things committed to his care," he "had brought no gain to his master, but had lost tens of thousands" — the steward of much who squandered all. Gill adds that the bringing of the debtor was not voluntary but forced "through the force of an awakened conscience, under guilt and terror," so that the staggering figure stands not merely for an external arithmetic of sin but for the crushing weight a roused conscience feels when God at last opens the book and brings the soul to account.
25:
"But forasmuch as he had not to pay, his lord commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and children, and all that he had" — the voices ground the detail in Hebrew law and read it of the sinner's helpless insolvency before God.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the legal warrant. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson all cite the law permitting debtors and their families to be sold into servitude (2 Kings 4:1; Leviticus 25:39; Exodus 22:3), Clarke noting the custom passed from the Jews to the Greeks and Romans and survives in the East, where "the sale of whole families, to discharge debts, is very common." Ellicott cautions against pressing the framework: drawn "from human laws," it indicates only "the sentence of condemnation passed upon the sinner himself," and "there is no occasion of pressing the details as we unfold the spiritual meaning."

The Reformed Gill and Calvin draw the doctrine. Gill makes every sinner an insolvent bankrupt who "has run out the whole stock of nature" with "nothing to offer by way of composition" — no works of righteousness can discharge a debt of sin, for God has a prior right to our obedience, and sin against an infinite God "contracts the nature of an infinite debt, which cannot be paid off by a finite creature"; Christ alone was able to pay it, and "has done it for his people." Calvin keeps the lesson within the parable's bounds: it shows "what will become of us, if God shall treat us with the utmost severity," and how necessary it then is to betake ourselves to prayer, "the only refuge that remains for transgressors"; the wide difference of the sums must be kept in view. The Alexandrian Origen, alone, reads the selling redemptively — sold along with his own, the active man might prosper under a new purchaser, by his worth "pay the whole debt in full," and "become the servant of his purchaser" — his usual upward turn. Wesley sums the legal background tersely — "such was the power which creditors anciently had over their insolvent debtors in several countries" — and the cloud is agreed that the sentence of sale, drawn from real human law, figures the just doom hanging over the sinner who, having nothing to pay, can only cast himself on mercy.
26:
"The servant therefore fell down, and worshipped him, saying, Lord, have patience with me, and I will pay thee all" — the voices agree the "worship" is the prostrate homage of a subject, and read the servant's vow as the natural, if vain, impulse of the awakened sinner.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the gesture. Barnes insists it is not religious homage but, in "a humble, and reverent, and earnest manner," the prostration customary before an eastern king (Matthew 2:2); Ellicott and Clarke agree it is "the prostrate homage of a servant crouching before his master," Clarke rendering μακροθύμησον "be long-minded towards me — give me longer space." On the promise "I will pay thee all," Ellicott reads a keen spiritual diagnosis: though under such circumstances an idle boast, it "describes with singular aptness the first natural impulse" of one roused to the sinfulness of sin, who will "try to balance the account as by a series of instalments," scoring future righteous acts as a set-off against past transgressions — "in theological language, he seeks to be justified by works."

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke turns the scene into a pattern of penitence — deep humiliation of heart (he fell down), fervent prayer, confidence in mercy ("have patience"), and a firm purpose to devote soul and body to God — and, more boldly, finds the sinner truly able to "pay all" only when "he brings the sacrifice of the Lord Jesus to the throne of justice, by faith," offering an equivalent for the pardon he seeks. The Reformed Gill, by contrast, keeps the vow empty: the man, "not being able to stand before him," owns the debt and his present inability, his promise resting on no real ability of his own. The two read the same words from opposite ends — Clarke toward a faith-wrought payment in Christ, Gill toward a bankruptcy that only sovereign grace can meet — yet both hear in the prostration the posture of a soul thrown wholly on mercy. The grounded, shared note across the witnesses is that the servant's posture — flat on the ground, owning a debt he cannot meet — is the true attitude of the convicted sinner, whether his vow to "pay all" be read (with Clarke) as faith pleading Christ's merit or (with Gill) as an empty boast that only grace can answer; all agree that mercy, not the promise, is what saves him.
27:
"Then the lord of that servant was moved with compassion, and loosed him, and forgave him the debt" — the heart of the parable, where the voices find the free mercy of God to the helpless sinner.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the gratuity. Barnes and Clarke trace the forgiveness to "the eternal mercy of God" alone — "the source of salvation to a lost world," the tender pity that forgives freely — not to anything in the servant; Barnes again cautions against over-reading the parable's machinery, the earlier threat to sell wife and children being mere narrative consistency, "to keep up the story." Ellicott reads a precise theological sequence: the impotent effort at self-justification touches the heart of "the lord of that servant" and is "met with more than it asked for — not with patience and long-suffering only, but with the pity that forgives freely"; so far as the man believes his Lord's assurance, "he is now justified by faith," and the noun for "debt" (a contracted loan, δάνειον) suggests, as in the parables of the Pounds, the Talents, and the Unjust Steward, that what we call our own — life with all its opportunities — "is really lent to us, and God requires repayment with interest." Robertson likewise glosses τὸ δάνειον as "the loan," the interest having swelled the debt enormously.

The Reformed Gill presses the point hardest: the lord was moved "not by any actions of his, as his prostrating himself… nor by his cries and entreaties, nor by his promises, which were not at all to be depended on, but by his own goodness and will" — "to the pure mercy and free grace of God is to be ascribed what is after related." The voices are here at one, sounding the single note of unmerited, sovereign compassion (Psalm 78:38), the God who "being full of compassion, forgave their iniquity." Clarke, with Barnes, will not let the free pardon be obscured: "this is the source of salvation to a lost world, the tender pity, the eternal mercy of God," moved by nothing in the debtor; and the cloud stands wholly together here, the Reformed and the grammatical and the wesleyan alike confessing that the cancelling of so vast a debt springs from the lord's compassion alone and not from any deserving in the man.
28:
"But the same servant went out, and found one of his fellowservants, which owed him an hundred pence… and took him by the throat" — the voices feel the shock of the contrast and weigh the small sum against the great.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the comparison. Barnes and Clarke reckon the hundred denarii at a few pounds, "a very small sum compared with what had been forgiven to the first servant," teaching, Barnes says, that the offences our fellow-men commit against us are trifling beside our offences against God, and that since God has forgiven us so much we ought to forgive the small. Yet Ellicott resists a pure antithesis: the "hundred pence" were a hundred days' wages, enough to feed thousands, "a really considerable sum" to Galilean fishermen, so that the parable "recognised that the offences which men commit against their brothers may, in themselves, be many and grievous enough" — trifling only when set against our sins against God. Robertson surveys the translators' various renderings (from "fifty shillings" to "twenty dollars") and notes the imperfect ἔπνιγεν, "began to choke or throttle him," a seizure by the throat the Roman law allowed, citing Livy and Cicero on the twisted neck.

On the brutal outrage Ellicott presses the searching question the verse forces: can a man truly pardoned and justified become thus merciless? "The experience of every age, almost of every household, shows that the inconsistency is but too fatally common." The man "is not consciously a hypocrite, but he is as yet 'double minded' (James 1:8), and the baser self is not conquered"; in the later language of the New Testament his faith is not one which "worketh by love" (Galatians 5:6) — he is justified, but not as yet sanctified. Clarke marks how the man, fresh from forgiveness, "immediately" abused the liberty his lord had accorded him, "while the favour he had received still sounded in his ears." The grounded, shared lesson is the violence of the contrast — a man fresh from the remission of millions seizing a fellow for a few pounds — which the witnesses take as the very anatomy of an unforgiving heart, and which Ellicott and Clarke alike use to probe how a soul that has tasted mercy can yet refuse it, finding the answer not in conscious hypocrisy but in a love not yet ruling the will.
29:
"And his fellowservant fell down at his feet, and besought him, saying, Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all" — the comment is brief, the voices struck chiefly by the exact mirroring of the first servant's own plea.

The Reformed Gill marks that the fellow-servant begged "in the most humble and submissive manner, just as he himself had done a little before at the feet of his Lord" — the very posture and words that had won the first servant his release now spurned by him. The repetition is the point: he is asked for precisely the mercy he had received.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws out the difference that sharpens the guilt. Recalling the first servant's identical petition in v.26, he notes that here the promise was "not a vain pretence" — "a few weeks or months of labour would have enabled the debtor to pay what he thus owed," whereas the ten thousand talents could never be paid; "man can atone for his offences as against man, though not as against God." The small, genuinely payable debt makes the refusal the more monstrous. Clarke confines himself to textual notes, observing that the clause "at his feet" and the word "all" (πάντα) are wanting in several manuscripts and dropped by some editors. The shared and grounded note is simply this: the merciless creditor was begged, in his debtor's very words, for a mercy that lay easily within his power to grant. Albert Barnes simply refers the reader back to the preceding verse (v.28), treating the fellow-servant's plea as the deliberate echo it is. The witnesses, few and brief here, are agreed that the parable's whole moral hangs on this mirroring: the debtor of a few pence kneels in the identical posture and pleads in the identical words by which the debtor of ten thousand talents had so lately been spared, so that the refusal which follows is laid bare as a self-condemning thing — the man denies to another exactly what, on the same terms, he had himself received. Clarke's note that 'at his feet' and 'all' drop out of some manuscripts touches only the wording, not this shared and weighty sense.
30:
"And he would not: but went and cast him into prison, till he should pay the debt" — the voices read the flat refusal as the parable's turning-point and the very type of the unforgiving heart.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both the persistence and the folly of the act. Robertson marks the imperfect ἤθελεν, "persistent refusal," and the futuristic aorist of "till he should pay," the man acting "on the instinct of a base nature, and also doubtless in accordance with long habits of harsh tyrannical behaviour towards men in his power." Ellicott observes the vindictiveness is even "suicidal": as a slave he could not have his fellow-slave sold, only imprisoned, and "in so doing he cuts the debtor off from all opportunities of gaining the money by which he might pay his debt" — patience would have enabled restitution, while "harshness shuts him up as in the prison of a sullen defiance," a lesson, he adds, with its analogue in the spiritual life.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws the searching moral, quoting Portia's plea for mercy: "to the unmerciful, God will show no mercy; this is an eternal purpose of the Lord, which never can be changed." God "teaches us what to do to a fellow-sinner by what He does to us," our fellow-servant's debt to us and ours to God being as a hundred pence to ten thousand talents (Matthew 6:12); when we are freely forgiven so mighty a sum, to exact recompense for the most trifling fault is monstrous. Gill notes simply that he "would not" have patience or forbear, as he had himself been forborne, and Wesley that he "protested he should lie there, till he should pay the whole debt." Ellicott adds that this vindictiveness "is not without its analogue in the interpretation of the parable": where patience and forbearance "at once encourage and enable the offender to make restitution," harshness only shuts him up, as in a prison, in "a sullen defiance" — so that the unforgiving spirit defeats the very end it claims to seek, and injures itself along with its victim.
31:
"So when his fellowservants saw what was done, they were very sorry, and came and told their lord all that was done" — the voices caution against over-reading the detail, while drawing from it the witness of conscience and community.

The grammatical-historical commentators restrain the allegory. Barnes insists the verse is "a mere circumstance thrown into the story" to keep it consistent — not that we should literally "go and tell God," who already knows all the actions of his children — and warns against "abusing the Bible, and departing from the design of parables, to press every circumstance"; the one great truth is the duty of forgiveness and the evil of refusing it. Robertson notes διεσάφησαν, "made wholly clear" to their lord, and observes "that is the usual result in the long run. There is a limit to what people will put up with." Ellicott reads the fellow-servants as members of the same spiritual society and dwells on their mood: not anger or indignation, though such would be natural, but sorrow — "sorrow, rather than anger, is the mood of the true disciple of Christ as he witnesses the sins against love which are the scandals of the Christian society," anger belonging rather to the Lord and Judge (v.32).

The Reformed Calvin agrees we ought not to search for mystery here, "because it contains nothing but what nature teaches": yet the men who live among us "will be so many witnesses against us before God," for cruelty cannot but excite their displeasure and hatred, every man fearing the like may fall on his own head. On the clause following he adds that it is foolish to ask how God punishes sins he has already forgiven — "the simple meaning is this: though he offers mercy to all, yet severe creditors, from whom no forgiveness can be obtained, are unworthy of enjoying it." The Latin Remigius alone allegorizes the fellow-servants as the angels or the faithful preachers of the Church, who "in spirit" tell their Lord by showing the sorrow of their hearts over a brother's perdition. Their coming to tell the lord is, says Ellicott, the appeal Christ makes by anticipation to the judgment Christians at large — perhaps mankind itself — would pass on such conduct, a judgment of grief more than of wrath.
32:
"Then his lord, after that he had called him, said unto him, O thou wicked servant, I forgave thee all that debt, because thou desiredst me" — the brief comments register the summons to judgment and the conscience struck dumb.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke catches the drama in three strokes: "Alas! how shall he appear! Confounded. What shall he answer? He is speechless!" — the silence of the arraigned servant standing, as Remigius noted on the previous verse, for the truth that in the day of judgment "all excusing of ourselves shall be cut off." Ellicott refines the wording, preferring "entreatedst me" for the verb, and presses a tender point: the man had not specifically asked to have the whole debt cancelled; "his general prayer for forbearance had been answered above all that he could ask or think" — the very greatness of the grace he received now measures the depth of his ingratitude.

The Reformed Gill notes only that the lord "ordered him to be called, and brought before him" — the same king who at first "did not call him wicked, nor did he at all chide him, but had mercy on him" (so Chrysostom on v.27) now names him "wicked" precisely because kindness had not mended him. The shared and grounded sense is plain: a servant forgiven beyond his asking, when his cruelty is exposed, stands without a word; the chapter's whole weight now bears down on him in the king's reproach, whose force the next verse (v.33) unfolds. The shared and grounded sense across the witnesses is plain: a servant forgiven far beyond his asking, when his cruelty is dragged to light, can offer no word in his own defence, the silence itself standing (as Remigius observed) for the day when "all excusing of ourselves shall be cut off"; and the king's address — "O thou wicked servant" — names as wicked the very man he had pitied, because, as Chrysostom saw on v.27, kindness had failed to mend him and only judgment remains. The reproach's full logic the next verse unfolds.
33:
"Shouldest not thou also have had compassion on thy fellowservant, even as I had pity on thee?" — the voices hear the cutting reproach that exposes the whole point of the parable: received mercy is the measure and the obligation of mercy given.

The grammatical-historical commentators feel its force. Robertson: "The king fits the cap on this wicked slave that he put on the poor debtor" — οὐκ ἔδει σε, "was it not necessary?" Ellicott reads the comparison "even as I had pity on thee" as designed to lead the disciples to the true meaning of the prayer they had been taught — "Forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors" (Matthew 6:12) — so that the unforgiving servant becomes the very picture of one who prays that petition and contradicts it by his life. Gill states the reasonable expectation plainly: it is "what ought to be, and may be expected, that such who have received mercy should show mercy," the least return being to follow the example set him.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke sharpens the rebuke into a pointed antithesis: "It became Me to show mercy, when thou didst earnestly entreat me, because I am merciful. It became thee also to have shown mercy, because thou wert so deep in debt thyself, and hadst obtained mercy" (οὐκ ἔδει καὶ σέ, "did it not become thee also?"). The whole company is here at one, with no dissent: the mercy a man has freely received from God lays on him the plainest and most binding obligation to deal mercifully with his brother, and the failure to do so is both unreasonable and wicked. Jamieson-Fausset-Brown add only a cross-pointer to the parallel teaching; the verse's single, shared note needs no debate among the witnesses. Robertson's image — the king fitting upon the wicked slave the very cap he had set upon his poor debtor — captures what every witness here sees: that the servant is condemned out of his own conduct, judged by the measure he himself had used, so that the verse becomes the living illustration of "with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again," and the surest exposition of the clause in the Lord's Prayer that binds our forgiveness of others to God's forgiveness of us.
34:
"And his lord was wroth, and delivered him to the tormentors, till he should pay all that was due unto him" — the voices weigh the "tormentors" and debate whether the sentence is temporal, eternal, or purgatorial.

The grammatical-historical commentators keep close to the imagery. Barnes takes the "tormentors" (βασανισταί) as the keepers of the prison and the punishment as imprisonment till payment, not literal torture, since "torments were inflicted on criminals, not on debtors." Robertson, citing papyri uses of βασανίζω for the torture of slaves and Livy's old centurion shown to a workhouse with his back scarred, allows real torture but insists the point is that "he could never pay back that vast debt" — the clause is "not purgatorial, but punitive." Ellicott reads the "tormentors" as deliberately vague symbols of "whatever agencies God employs in the work of righteous retribution, the stings of remorse, the scourge of conscience, the scorn and reproach of men," not excluding what lies "behind the veil"; the words at once suggest a limit and the impossibility of ever reaching it — how could the man in the tormentors' hands earn the ten thousand talents? — leaving us "in silent awe" before "the things that are impossible with man are possible with God" (Matthew 19:26).

Here a sharp debate divides the confessions. Calvin states it flatly: "The Papists are very ridiculous in endeavoring to light the fire of purgatory by the word till," for Christ points "not temporal death… but eternal death," the debt being unpayable. The wesleyan Wesley draws the opposite-leaning warning — against the assurance that a pardon once granted can never be retracted: the debtor "was freely and fully forgiven; he wilfully and grievously offended; his pardon was retracted, the whole debt required, and the offender delivered to the tormentors for ever," with no hope of release, for "this he could never do." Clarke softens the picture toward conscience, reading the tortures as "the horrors of a guilty conscience, and… a fearful looking for of fiery indignation," the worm that dieth not. Barnes again restrains the literal reading even while the others press the eternal weight.
35:
"So likewise shall my heavenly Father do also unto you, if ye from your hearts forgive not every one his brother their trespasses" — the parable's application, on which the voices stand unanimous: forgiveness must be heart-deep, or God's mercy is forfeited.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the moral with care. Barnes lists the chain of truths the verse seals — our sins are great; God freely forgives them; the offences committed against us are comparatively small; therefore we should freely forgive; and if we do not, God will justly be angry and punish us — insisting "from your hearts" means "not merely in words, but really and truly to feel and act towards him as if he had not offended us" (Proverbs 21:13; James 2:13). Robertson sums it crisply: "No sham or lip pardon, and as often as needed" — Christ's full reply to Peter's question of v.21, a parable "surely needed today." Clarke turns it into a thunderous personal appeal to any who refuse mercy to a penitent child or insolvent debtor: "How then canst thou ever expect to see the face of the just and merciful God?" Both Clarke and Ellicott note the closing words "their trespasses" are textually doubtful, likely added to conform the verse to Matthew 6:14–15.

Ellicott presses the verse against false security: it "cuts through the meshes of many theological systems by which men have deceived themselves" — the self-assurance of justification, the absolving words of the priest, taken as final and irreversible — for "the debt may come back," since "if faith does not work by love, it ceases to justify," the characters of the discharge traced as in sympathetic ink that fades with the cooling of love. The Reformed Gill keeps the warning within its proper object — "such who are cruel and hard hearted to their brethren, and are of merciless and unforgiving spirits" — on whom God acts in kind. The whole cloud here speaks with one voice: the measure of mercy we show is the measure we shall receive, and the forgiveness God requires is of the heart, not of the lips alone.
